summary: This conversation explains caching as a method to speed up website loading. It covers what caching is, where it can be implemented (browser, database/server, server/client), how to decide where to cache based on performance bottlenecks, what data to cache (frequently accessed), and common tools like Redis and CDNs.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: My website is taking forever to load. How can I speed it up?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right. Are you doing any caching?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: The concept of caching is simple. You basically store information closer to the user so they can get it faster. Your browser does this all the time. For example, the first time you visit a website, it probably takes a long time, but the second time you visit it, it usually loads a lot faster because your browser is saving things like the images and the static files from that site locally.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool. If the browser is doing it for me, then what do I need to do?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: The browser is just one of many places where caching can happen. You could add a cache between your database and your server or even between your server and your clients.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, then where should I cache?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, that depends on a lot of factors, but a good place to start is looking at what part of the request process is taking up a lot of time. In this simple example, if your entire request is taking 10 seconds and 8 seconds of that is the database, then a good place to start the caching would be between the database and the server.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, I just cache everything in there?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: You only want to cache your most frequently accessed data. For example, a popular YouTube video. When Mr. Beast drops a video, do you want every single request to watch it going all the way to your database? No, you just want to bring it into your cache once and then every single request can hit this much faster.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, it makes sense. What tools do you use to actually implement this?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Redis is the most popular choice for application level caching, and you can also cache using a CDN, using something like CloudFront or Cloudflare, which I'll talk about.
